Metrics Localization positive micro F1 (pmF1): For datapoint with predicted masks {mi}N i=1 and ground-truth masks { ˆmj}M j= , we compute the IoU matrix IoUij = IoU(mi, ˆmj). (14) We then compute an optimal bipartite assignment (Hungarian matching) σ maximizing total IoU. For an IoU threshold τ , prediction is true positive if it is matched and IoUi,σ(i) τ ; otherwise it is false positive. Unmatched ground-truth masks are false negatives. Let Pτ , Pτ , and Nτ denote the resulting counts. We compute micro-F1 at threshold τ by accumulating these counts across all positive datapoints (those with > 0), pmF1τ = 2 Pτ,total 2 Pτ,total + Pτ,total + Nτ,total . Following SAM3, we evaluate at 10 thresholds τ {0.50, 0.55, . . . , 0.95} and average: pmF1 = 1 10 τ pmF1@τ . (15) (16) We also report the per-threshold values (e.g., pmF1@0.50 and pmF1@0. ) when relevant. Classification image-level MCC (IL_MCC): We adopt the same image-level presence classification metric as SAM3: we treat each datapoint as binary decision (object present vs. not present) based on whether the model predicts any mask, and summarize the resulting confusion matrix with the Matthews Correlation Coefficient (IL_MCC). We refer readers to SAM3 for the exact definition and implementation details. Combined classification-gated F1 (cgF1): Combining localization and classification: cgF1 = pmF1 IL_MCC. (17) In addition to micro aggregation, we report macro variant Macro-F1 (per-sample): computed as the mean of per-sample scores. For positive samples, the per-sample score is the mean local F1 across thresholds; for true negatives (no ground truth and no prediction) the per-sample score is set to 1, while false positives and false negatives receive 0. This metric highlights per-image stability rather than being dominated by high-mask-count samples. B. Architecture Study central design question for unified dense-prediction architectures is how to distribute model capacity between depth (number of layers L) and width (hidden dimension dmodel). Because visual features and autoregressive task tokens share the same transformer stack, there is risk of modality crowding: the channel bandwidth may become bottleneck when both modalities compete for representational capacity. We investigate this through two controlled studies on the SaCo benchmark, evaluating bounding-box detection at an IoU threshold of 0.75 and reporting the average MCC and Macro F1 across all seven splits after 60k training steps. 33 Falcon-Perception Table A.1: Metrics averaged over the seven SaCo splits at 60k steps. dmodel MCC Macro F1 46.4 20 48.2 20 49.8 20 20 53.2 52.4 55.7 57.5 58.5 61.5 60.9 384 448 512 768 896 Figure A.1: Impact of architectural choices: Width scaling at fixed depth (L=20). Table A.2: Metrics averaged over the seven SaCo splits at 60k steps. dmodel MCC Macro F1 52.4 72 50.0 54 42 54.0 53.2 20 50.8 14 60.5 58.8 61.9 61.5 59. 384 448 512 768 896 Figure A.2: Impact of architectural choices: Iso-compute depthwidth trade-off. Hyperparameter transfer: To ensure that performance differences reflect architectural capacity rather than optimization artifacts, we adopt the Maximal Update Parameterization (µP) (Yang, 2019) for learning-rate transfer across widths. We first tune the optimal learning rate on reference configuration (20L / dmodel=768) and transfer it to every other width via lrtarget = lrref dtarget dref , (18) so that the effective magnitude of weight updates remains consistent regardless of hidden dimension. All other hyperparameters are kept identical across configurations. B.1 Width Scaling at Fixed Depth We fix the network depth at L=20 layers and vary the hidden dimension across dmodel {384, 448, 512, 768, 896}. Results are summarized in Table A.1 and Figure A.1. Performance improves monotonically from dmodel=384 (MCC = 46.4, F1 = 55.7) to dmodel=768 (MCC = 53.2, F1 = 61.5), gain of +6.8 MCC and +5.8 Macro F1. Increasing width further to 896 yields slight regression (MCC = 52.4), indicating that at 20 layers the architecture saturates around dmodel 768. The sharp degradation at small widths is consistent with capacity-bottleneck effect: when visual and language features must share narrow hidden state, neither modality is adequately represented. B.2 DepthWidth Trade-off at Fixed Compute To disentangle width from overall scale, we fix an approximate compute budget of 300M parameters and sweep the aspect ratio across five configurations spanning 5 range in depth. Results are shown in Table A.2 and Figure A.2. The two balanced configurations: 42L / 512 34 Falcon-Perception (MCC = 54.0, F1 = 61.9) and 20L / 768 (MCC = 53.2, F1 = 61.5) achieve the highest scores and lie within one MCC point of each other. Both substantially outperform the extremes: the deepest-narrowest variant (72L / 384: MCC = 52.4) and the shallowest-widest variant (14L / 896: MCC = 50.8) each trail the best configuration by 24 points. Notably, the 54L / 448 model (MCC = 50.0) underperforms 72L / 384 despite having wider hidden state, suggesting that this particular depthwidth combination falls into an unfavorable operating regime. B.3 Discussion Both studies converge on two findings: 1. Width is necessary but not sufficient: Study shows that widening the hidden dimension from 384 to 768 yields +6.8 MCC improvement at fixed depth, confirming that sufficient channel capacity is critical for dense prediction in unified stack. However, gains saturate beyond dmodel 768. 2. Balanced aspect ratios are optimal under iso-compute: Study shows that, for fixed compute budget, moderate configurations (42L / 512 and 20L / 768) outperform both deep-narrow and shallow-wide extremes by 24 MCC. This indicates that representational diversity from depth and channel capacity from width are independently necessary and neither alone compensates for deficit in the other. Taken together, these results suggest that the unified single-stack architecture does not inherently suffer from modality crowding. The optimal operating point in our explored range is 42L / 512, though 20L / 768 offers competitive alternative at shallower depth that may be preferable when inference latency is consideration. C. Qualitative Analysis C.1 Image Feature Quality Here, we assess the quality of the image features output by our model, which employs early fusion of the image and text tokens. Figure A.3 shows the PCA maps of the image features at the Falcon Perception output layer (before the AnyUp feature upsampler) and after being upsampled by the upsampler. We observe that our model learns to distinguish between objects in the image to reasonable extent, as evident in the image features before upsampling (e.g., the coffee cup, notebook and pen in the fourth row on the left are clearly distinguishable in the image features before upsampling). Furthermore, at the feature upsampler output, the image features are enhanced with high-fidelity and the instances are better visible, indicating that the features output by our model before upsampling encompass sufficient scene information that is required for promptable instance segmentation. These results show the effectiveness of early fusion design in our Falcon Perception. C.2 Varying the upsampling factor Here, we qualitatively analyze the effect of the upsampling factor on the mask quality. We compare the output instance masks generated with different impact factors in Figure A.4. Our Falcon Perception uses patch size of 16 for the image patchification, resulting in 16 downscaled image features at the model output before mask computation. In order to increase the resolution of the image features, we use the AnyUp upsampler, whose upscaling factor can be varied to compute masks at different image resolutions. Note that the final masks are bilinearly interpolated to image size, for upsampling factors less than 16. As baseline, we use bilinear upsampling (16) technique (left column in Figure A.4), which results in masks that have holes inside objects and artifacts outside. The effectiveness of using the feature upsampler is clearly seen even when the upscaling factor is set to 1 (second column from left), where the mask quality 35 Falcon-Perception Figure A.3: PCA maps before and after upsampling: Image features before upsampling already distinguish multiple objects, e.g., the coffee cup, notebook, and pen in the fourth row on the left. Similarly, chopsticks along with various food items in second row on the right and the four cups in the bottom right row can be distinguished. The feature upsampler enhances these representations, making instances more visible, suggesting that the pre-upsampling features capture sufficient scene information for instance segmentation and demonstrating the effectiveness of the early-fusion design. significantly improves over the baseline. Furthermore, as the factor is increased, we observe mask refinement with improvements at the instance boundaries. This is noticeable in the first row of the figure, where the boundaries of the fourth apple instance from left improves for upsampling factor 8. Similarly, in the third row of the figure, the mask of left penguin is incomplete with its beak missing partially for factors 1 to 4, while it is accurate for higher factors. C.3 Qualitative Comparison with SAM Figures A.5-A.10 show the qualitative comparison of the predicted masks between our Falcon Perception and SAM 31 on various image-prompt pairs with prompts varying from Level 0 to 4. Overall, SAM 3 incorrectly predicts false positives when query prompts requiring visual text recognition (Figure A.7), complex expression prompts are used (Figures A.8 and A.9) and is limited by the number of query tokens in its DETR decoder for dense predictions (Figure A.10). In contrast, our Falcon Perception can read text reasonably well, decipher complex expressions and predict the correct instances among other distractors in the scene. Furthermore, since our models predictions are autoregressive in nature, once trained with such long-contexts, it can easily scale the detections to many instances (up to 600). These results show the added capabilities for our dense transformer based early-fusion perception model. 1While we acknowledge that SAM 3 cannot reliably predict masks for levels 2 to 4 since it is not trained in that manner, we compare with it to showcase the added capability of our Falcon Perception in this regard. 36 Falcon-Perception Figure A.4: Impact of the upsampling factor during inference: Bilinearly upsampling the image features at the perception model output (16x downsampled) and computing the instance masks results in degraded quality (leftmost column) with holes inside the instances and mask regions outside the instances.
